data.title: Gold Still Sets The Tone For Silver's Next Move
data.description:
Intro

I’m likely not the only one who has been eyeing silver’s impressive chart lately and wondering how I missed a move that now seems so obvious. But as we know, charts always look plain and simple in hindsight. In the middle of the actual action, however, there’s a mix of emotions and all sorts of confusing signals that often make it hard to stay objective and pull the trigger at the right time.

I have to admit, I’ve long been more excited about gold—viewing it as the ultimate symbol of money and wealth since ancient times. I’ve delved deep into its dynamics, even writing an extensive analysis last November: From Ancient Times To Bretton Woods And On To Bitcoin: Why Gold Still Matters. However, I’ve never examined the drivers behind silver’s movements in more detail. Given the recent dramatic surge to all-time highs and the once-unthinkable breach of the $100 mark, I felt this was the right moment to unpack silver's perspectives.

My goal is to understand whether the current setup—given that silver has fallen nearly 40% from its all-time highs—is more like a consolidation before another leg higher, or if it’s reasonable to expect an extended drawdown and stay on the sidelines this time.

Silver Is Tightly Correlated with Gold

For me, silver has always been something of a satellite to gold—or its younger brother, if you will. Throughout history, silver coins have played a crucial role whenever kings and rulers of the era sought to expand the monetary base once their vaults had been emptied of gold. In a sense, they have been fellow money equivalents for centuries. One could even say that silver has historically served as a vehicle for "Quantitative Easing" (QE) for those in power, much like the debasement of silver denarii in the Roman Empire when funds ran dry. When gold supply is tight, silver saves the day; I think it’s easy to draw various parallels to the present.

Latest Moves in Gold and Silver

I believe we can draw the starting line of the current gold (XAUUSD:CUR) rally to early 2024, when it decisively broke above $2,000 and, more importantly, managed to hold that level and begin its trend higher.

During this period, silver prices (XAGUSD:CUR) closely tracked the movements of gold, consistently aligning with its trends, until experiencing a significant surge at the end of 2025:

After reviewing past rallies, it appears typical for gold and silver to move in tandem—until silver suddenly surges ahead, often outpacing gold by a wide margin. This time, silver has outperformed gold by as much as nearly 100%.

Even a quick look at comparative charts going back to the 1980s shows the same pattern: while the two generally move in similar patterns, silver’s swings are far more aggressive, with much sharper bursts than gold.

We can identify the two most recent major cycles to examine more closely. The first, longer and stronger one began around 2008 during the subprime crisis and gradually cooled off by 2015. The second, milder one emerged during the COVID-era easing period, roughly from 2020 through 2023.

As seen in the first chart below, gold and silver initially moved mostly together in 2008. But at the start of 2009, gold’s rise accelerated while silver lagged behind for years before finally catching up during the QE‑driven surge in spring 2011. Silver then dramatically overshot gold — but the subsequent drawdown was just as sharp, with both metals eventually cooling off and spending the following years trending lower in nearly the same pattern.

The recent spike prior to the one we’re seeing now occurred roughly between 2020 and 2023. It followed almost the same pattern: in this cycle, silver started out stronger, gold eventually caught up and took the lead, silver followed with force, and then spiked ahead — even managing to stay above gold for a longer stretch. But just like in the previous cycle, both metals ultimately cooled off and ended the move by trending lower in sync.

…and here we have the current rally. Once again, the two traded nearly in sync until gold took the lead during last April’s tariff‑driven selloff. They then realigned, and silver surged toward the end of 2025. The cooling phase shown in the chart follows a very similar pattern so far.

To get an even stronger sense of just how massive the current rally has been, it’s useful to look at the next charts, which show the overwhelming surge in trading volumes that led to the all‑time highs in both gold and silver. The chart also shows that silver’s trading volumes have only recently risen to levels comparable to gold.

To add more context to these movements, it’s also worth taking a look at the gold and silver volatility chart, which shows that silver has almost always exhibited significantly higher volatility than gold, with only brief exceptions during 2000–2003 when the relationship was more mixed.

So to me, these cycles show that the two shiny metals are tightly correlated, but gold consistently plays the role of the older brother — the frontrunner, the one who sets the tone, if you will — as it has throughout human monetary history. When gold begins to move, it often takes some time for silver to catch up, but once it does, it tends to overshoot sharply. That overshoot is usually followed by a return to normality, with silver giving back its elevated price levels during easing phases. Even so, the broader picture remains the same: they generally move hand in hand — it’s just that the younger brother has a far more temperamental streak that reveals itself from time to time.

After the Recent Gold and Silver Rally — What Might Come Next

All of the above leads me to a simple conclusion: we can’t assess silver’s outlook in isolation. For silver to rally meaningfully from here, history’s playbook suggests it still needs a positive catalyst from gold’s price action. Silver has almost never staged a sustained rally without gold taking the strong lead.

What the Fundamentals Say

Many market observers point to silver’s real‑world physical demand as the basis for its elevated price action. According to the Silver Institute, the silver market is in a structural deficit:

The next graph shows where the demand is coming from. We can observe that as historical photographic demand has diminished, jewelry, electronics, and batteries have largely filled the gap. The trend appears encouraging and could support a continued rise in overall demand:

While it’s hard to find solid research that clearly and in detail proves, for example, that solar panels or other sectors will drive exponential growth in silver demand, I did come across one study that, in my view, provides credible evidence that the automotive industry will need more silver going forward. That said, we still can’t describe the demand outlook as explosive from any perspective.

The Silver Institute also offers a detailed breakdown of silver’s supply and demand:

As we can see, supply has increased in 2025 compared to 2024, while demand from real‑world industrial use and jewelry is on the decline. The only significant increase in demand is coming from the Coin & Net Bar segment. Setting that source of demand aside, it shows there’s roughly a 177‑million‑ounce surplus in terms of actual real‑world usage.

For me, it seems fair to conclude that, however we look at this supply‑and‑demand outline, silver’s price is not fully supported by real‑world consumption but is instead meaningfully influenced by investor and speculative sentiment.

Outlook for Silver in 2026

Silver Institute provides their view:

Total demand this year is forecast to fall modestly by 2 percent to 1.11 Boz. Double-digit losses are expected in jewelry and silverware, as the impact of higher prices continues. Industrial demand is projected to decline by 3 percent, chiefly due to a further and marked slowdown in PV offtake. Some of these losses will be mitigated by firmer coin and net bar demand, which is expected to jump by 18 percent.

Global silver mine production is expected to remain flat in 2026. Broader grade-related and operational pressures across key producing regions should offset modest growth at a limited number of assets. With mine production stable this year, we expect the structural market deficit to widen to 46.3 Moz.

To sum up, there’s nothing particularly encouraging here — real‑world usage is declining, and the demand story increasingly relies on investment flows.

Ways to Gain Exposure to Silver’s Price Moves

While there are plenty of ways for investors to get exposure to physical silver on an exchange, including through futures or ETFs like SLV, PSLV, or SIVR, there are also equity‑based alternatives. The Global X Silver Miners ETF (SIL) features a globally diversified mix of mining companies with strong exposure to silver. Amplify Junior Silver Miners ETF - (SILJ) is broadly similar but has a somewhat larger variety of miners and is more diversified. Sprott Silver Miners & Physical Silver ETF (SLVR) offers a blended approach, combining physical silver with an 18% stake in PSLV and exposure to silver mining companies as well.

The Global X Silver Miners ETF (SIL) Holdings:

Amplify Junior Silver Miners ETF (SILJ) Holdings:

Sprott Silver Miners & Physical Silver ETF (SLVR) Holdings:

For seasoned traders, there is also the ProShares Ultra Silver ETF (AGQ), which offers 2x leverage and provides additional upside optionality during silver’s wild price swings.

ProShares, a fund manager, presents its offering as follows:

AGQ seeks daily investment results, before fees and expenses, that correspond to two times (2x) the daily performance of the Bloomberg Silver SubindexSM.

For experienced traders, AGQ is a great vehicle to trade, delivering significantly more bang for the buck — but as a leveraged ETF, its risks can’t be ignored. The fund manager warns:

While the Fund has a daily investment objective, you may hold Fund shares for longer than one day if you believe it is consistent with your goals and risk tolerance. For any holding period other than a day, your return may be higher or lower than the Daily Target. These differences may be significant. Smaller index gains/losses and higher index volatility contribute to returns worse than the Daily Target. Larger index gains/losses and lower index volatility contribute to returns better than the Daily Target.

The more extreme these factors are, the more they occur together, and the longer your holding period while these factors apply, the more your return will tend to deviate. Investors should consider periodically monitoring their geared fund investments in light of their goals and risk tolerance.

AGQ invests in futures and swap and does not directly invest in silver.

Takeaway

Times are uncertain, and silver is undoubtedly an asset class that can serve both as a tool for portfolio diversification and—for those comfortable with sharper price swings—a vehicle for long-term capital preservation.

Silver’s real-world demand is clearly present, but the widely discussed structural deficit still depends more on investors' willingness to allocate capital than on a meaningful increase in industrial usage at the moment.

In my opinion, silver cannot be viewed in isolation from the broader investor appetite for gold, which has historically acted as the leading indicator for silver’s most decisive moves. In today’s geopolitically, monetarily, and fiscally uncertain environment, there are plenty of well-reasoned arguments supporting gold—and by extension, silver. That said, for silver to rally meaningfully from here, in my view, gold needs to re-establish its strong uptrend as an essential precondition.

As for my stance, I remain neutral—silver is a 'Hold' for me at the moment. History shows that after powerful spikes, it often moves sideways for a while or even drifts lower for an extended period. That said, for seasoned traders, silver continues to offer excellent opportunities for high-conviction setups.

data.title: Pinterest Is Likely Dead Money (Q1 Preview)
data.description:
Introduction & Investment Thesis

When I last wrote about Pinterest (PINS) at the end of December, I downgraded the stock to a “hold” as management guided for revenue to slow down in Q4, which tends to be their seasonally strongest time of the year. Meanwhile, a slowdown in the growth rate of ARPU (Average Revenue Per User) was also concerning to me, especially when far larger and deep-pocketed players like Meta (META) and Google (GOOG) have been growing their ad revenues at a much faster rate.

As I put it, “Pinterest management needs to present a bold new plan to revive growth back in the company, especially in its US & Canada regions. Until that happens, I believe investor sentiment will remain depressed and the stock price will be range-bound.”

That has exactly been the case, as the company failed to impress investors in their Q4 FY25 earnings in February, missing both top- and bottom-line estimates, from ARPU slowing down even further from weakness in ad pricing.

While management acknowledged their lackluster earnings and committed to broadening their advertiser base beyond large retail advertisers, which had slashed their ad budgets in the face of higher tariffs, they failed to provide a clear timeline as to when we can expect a potential inflection may occur.

Pinterest stock is down 23% since the time of my previous writing, hugely underperforming the S&P 500.

The company is slated to report its Q1 FY26 earnings on 05/04, where both revenue and earnings are expected to decline even further. The way I see it, even if Pinterest manages to beat guidance, I wouldn’t expect a durable rerating in the stock until we get clear evidence of top-line acceleration.

In the meantime, the stock does not even present an attractive risk-reward when looking at Wall Street price targets. As a result, in the absence of a clear timeline on potential revenue inflection, along with net-negative risk reward, I will downgrade the stock to a “sell” ahead of its Q1 earnings.

A Quick Recap Of Pinterest’s Q4 FY25

When Pinterest reported its Q4 FY25 earnings in February, the company missed both its revenue and earnings expectations.

On the revenue front, Pinterest saw its two-line growth rate slow 300 basis points sequentially to 14% YoY, generating $1.3B in revenue. This was the case, as the revenue growth rate in both Europe and Rest of World declined on a sequential basis. During the earnings call, management acknowledged their less than satisfactory results and attributed the weakness to tariffs affecting the ad budgets of major retailers, particularly in home furnishings and decor. CEO William Reddy also stressed the importance of broadening their revenue mix and discussed their three strategic priorities that are centered on “building a differential visual search, discovery, and shopping experience; keeping AI at the core of their product innovation strategy; and accelerating monetization through improved go-to-market and sales execution.”

The good news is that Pinterest’s MAUs (Monthly Active Users) continued to grow at a steady rate during the quarter, up 9% YoY to 619M users, with strength across all its geographies, as can be seen below.

However, the problem lies in the growth rate of ARPU, which was up just 2%, compared to 5% in Q3, as ad pricing weakened from their largest retail advertisers pulling back on their ad budgets.

In the meantime, the management is trying to compensate for their slowing growth by tightening their profitability parameters. As you can see below, the cost of revenue has been trending lower, along with R&D (Research & Development) and S&M (Sales & Marketing) expenses also declining as a percentage of revenue.

What investors want to see is an inflection in their ad revenue growth as management looks to diversify their advertisers by accelerating the growth in SMB and mid-market segments. Unfortunately, management failed to provide clarity on the timeline of their potential ad revenue inflection and margin implications of new investments.

What To Expect In Q1?

Looking forward, Pinterest is slated to report its Q1 FY26 earnings on 05/04, when revenue is expected to further slow to a 13.17% YoY growth rate during the quarter. What is even more concerning is that forward consensus estimates show no signs of inflection in revenue growth at any point in time in the coming quarters, as can be seen below.

Meanwhile, when it comes to earnings per share estimates, Pinterest is expected to see its Q1 EPS decline 4.6% YoY to $0.22 per share. But, based on consensus estimates, Q1 should mark a bottom, with the growth rate in earnings per share improving after that.

The competitive landscape is also not favorable to Pinterest, where far larger players like Meta and Google are projected to grow their ad revenues at a faster rate in FY26, by 24% and 12%, respectively, with superior margins, indicating significant market share loss and competitive moat erosion for Pinterest.

I’m Not Expecting Any Rerating Soon

There is no doubt that Pinterest stock is “cheap” at the moment, trading at a forward PE ratio of 11.07, 18% lower than the sector median and 63% lower than its 5-year average PE ratio.

However, until we see a meaningful reacceleration in the company’s top-line growth, I don’t expect a meaningful re-rating in the stock. In other words, Pinterest is likely going to be “dead money” until then.

Plus, I look at Wall Street price target estimates; the risk-reward does not look attractive, despite the stock trading at record low valuations. At the moment, the average price target on Wall Street is $23.43, representing a potential upside of 18% from current levels. However, when looking at the “low” price target of $15.40 per share, there is a downside risk of 22% from current levels.

In other words, investing at current levels does not present a net positive risk-reward, along with management providing no guidelines of potential inflection timelines, thus further depressing investor sentiment in the stock.

My final verdict and conclusions

Pinterest is likely “dead money” for now. As a previous investor, I got out of the name in January at a loss of 22% and will stay away from the stock, as the company has clearly lost the plot at the moment, while risk-reward is not attractive either.

Meanwhile, I will downgrade the stock to a “sell” ahead of its Q1 earnings.

data.title: Brinker: Oil Prices A Lower Risk As Chili's Boom Continues In Q3
data.description:
Shares of Brinker International (EAT) have been a poor performer over the past year. While the company has actually reported strong results thanks to a remarkable turnaround at its Chili’s brand, investors have soured on restaurant stocks over fears about discretionary spending. Strong quarterly earnings reported Wednesday morning may help to dispel these fears, and shares jumped 10% in early trading. I last covered Brinker in January, but this call has not played out with shares down about 8% since then. At the time, I saw the outlook brightening for restaurants, but the Iran War has thrown this into doubt. With updated financials, now is a good time to revisit EAT.

In the company’s third quarter, Brinker earned $2.90, which beat estimates by $0.04 as revenue grew 3% to $1.5 billion. EPS grew 9% from last year thanks to income growth and the benefits of its buyback program. Same-restaurant sales continue to perform fairly well, up 3.3%, though there remains a large divergence between brands. Management described demand as “strong,” pointing to a stronger consumer than other companies like Domino’s (DPZ).

Adjusted EBITDA rose about 1.5% to $224 million, and operating income was up 6% to $167 million. Operating margins expanded by 30bps to 11.3%, thanks primarily to lower corporate costs. Restaurant-level EBITDA margins actually declined by 50bps to 18.4%, given one-time repair expenses and higher labor costs. Operating margins benefit from lower corporate overhead as a share of revenue thanks to ongoing growth of the top line, a margin benefit that should persist into fiscal 2027.

Chili's leads; Maggiano lags

Drilling into segment results, Chili’s same-restaurant sales rose a solid 4%, a 20th straight quarter of growth. With $1.36 billion of sales, Chili’s is over 90% of the business, so its performance is what matters most to EAT. Comparable sales growth is decelerating here after a period of double-digit growth, reflecting a much more difficult comparison. Last year, comparable sales jumped 31%, and that pace of growth is not sustainable at a restaurant (eventually you run out of tables and hit capacity). Indeed, growing further on top of last year’s growth is very impressive.

There has been concern that the jump in oil prices and geopolitical uncertainty would lead consumers to pull back. While a definite risk, this is not happening. Comps were up just 0.6% in January (weighed down by a winter storm), and then they rose 5.9% in both February and March when oil prices began their surge. Chili’s has returned to the forefront of consumers’ minds, and its value pricing has helped it weather this shock so far.

Maggiano’s continues to struggle as Brinker is in the early innings of its turnaround plan, bringing this chain back to its roots, similar to what it did for Chili’s. Comparable sales fell 4.6%. This brand is just $107 million in sales, so it is less consequential to results. Operating income fell 60% to $5 million due to lower sales and margin pressure. Maggiano’s has brought back old favorites to the menu, refocused on service quality, and enhanced portions. Just as these efforts worked to turn around Chili’s to remarkable success, I expect Maggiano’s to bottom later this year, though I expect the pace of recovery to be slower.

Macro was improving before Iran

Looking at the macro backdrop, my view entering the year was that the macro environment was improving for restaurants, at least before the Iran War. This seems to have been playing out. As you can see below, restaurant sales inflected higher in February with 61% of respondents reporting growth, the highest reading in over a year. Larger tax refunds should be a tailwind for consumer spending in 2026, all else equal, and higher grocery prices have made restaurant pricing relatively more attractive. The question is to what extent oil derails this story; I believe the impact is less than feared.

Gas prices are a manageable risk

While there is this perceived risk that gasoline prices will hurt spending at restaurants, the data actually does not back that up. Looking from 2000-2020 (COVID restaurant closures and reopening heavily distort the 2020-2022 period), it is interesting to see that spikes in oil prices in 2003, 2007, 2010, and 2017 do not coincide with drops in restaurant spending. If anything, spending at the pump and at restaurants generally move in the same direction, with one notable exception. During 2014-2015, when oil prices plunged, consumers used those savings to spend more at restaurants.

This is an interesting phenomenon that is comforting when considering the outlook for Brinker with oil prices above $100 (and likely to remain elevated for some time in my view). Now, increased gas prices force consumers to either save less or spend less on other items. The outcome likely depends on the income level of the consumer; higher-income consumers with greater savings and buffers are likely to just save less, and the pressure is likely to be more pronounced among lower-income consumers, a cohort that already spends less at restaurants on average.

I see travel as facing more pressure, and consumers may actually use those savings to spend a bit more while at home, providing support for restaurant traffic. I also expect consumers to prioritize value when going out, and Brinker’s brands are well positioned for this possibility. The impact of higher oil prices on consumer spending remains a key risk to monitor, but given historic data, it may not be as concerning as first thought.

Cash flow enables capital returns

Turning back to Brinker, so far this year, the company has generated just under $400 million of free cash flow, despite $10 million of working capital pressure. This is up from $308 million last year, thanks to stronger profitability. During the quarter, EAT repaid the balance on its revolver, and it also bought back $108 million of stock. Thanks to buybacks, its share count is down about 4% from last year. The company has a stellar balance sheet with just $424 million of long-term finance leases and debt. Given this balance sheet, Brinker can direct all free cash flow to shareholder returns in the form of buybacks.

Alongside results, management tightened EPS guidance to the upper end of the prior range, at $10.60-$10.85, while lowering its cap-ex outlook by $10 million to about $245 million. Back in January, I was targeting at least $10.10 in EPS and $450 million in free cash flow. This view was a bit too conservative, and I expect EPS to be in the upper half of the guided range while free cash flow should be at least $475 million, depending on working capital movements.

Shares have upside past $160

Looking forward to fiscal 2027, it is still early, but I expect mid-single-digit growth at Chili’s, low single-digit declines at Maggiano’s, and about a 5% decline in the share count. This should support 10-15% growth in earnings or $12-$12.50 in EPS. Even at $140, shares are less than 12x forward earnings when peers like Darden (DRI) trade closer to 18x. Brinker is smaller and less diversified, but its primary brand has significant momentum.

As investors come to appreciate that oil prices are not as problematic as feared, we should see shares re-rate higher. I believe shares should trade at least to a 7% forward free cash flow yield, which still discounts just 3% long-term growth to generate a 10% return. As such, I would be a buyer of EAT up to $160, and I view this target as conservative. With over 15% of further upside, I reiterate EAT as a “buy.”

data.title: Pulse Seismic Inc: A Stronger Yielder Benefitting From Increasing Western Canadian Activity
data.description:
Pulse Seismic Inc. (PSD:CA) is the owner of the largest bank of 2D and 3D seismic data in Western Canada. The primary users of this data are oil and gas companies, looking to gain information on new areas, reinterpretations of existing structures and new crown mineral locations, among others. The O&G industry has come a long way since the wildcatting days, with a focus on more efficient outcomes both from exploration and producing wells. Seismic data has helped to dramatically improve the efficiency, giving users of the data better detail on a large scale and with precision when required. Pulse Seismic has developed a substantial library of this data in a fossil fuel rich area that it has been able to monetize through several different mechanisms. This article looks at Pulse Seismic’s business as well as the current macro environment it is operating in.

The Company’s Seismic Data

The company has both 2D and 3D seismic data in its library. 2D gives a wider mapping level to get an idea of geological formations. 3D data goes into further detail than 2D data but is focused on a smaller area. The company largely built its database by acquiring the data during the 2010s with a series of acquisitions.

The value proposition for customers is quite clear; in the past, they may have had to do all their own data gathering for each property a customer may be investigating for economic viability. Rather than needing their own equipment and specialty, they can rent the data only for the relevant area needed. This saves the customer a lot of money. Similarly, Pulse Seismic can then monetize the data as often or for as long as a customer has interest in the properties. While most Pulse’s customers are in the O&G industry, it is not limited to that with customers in several other industries utilizing the data including but not limited to lithium and geothermal.

One of the drivers of use of Pulse’s data is land transactions. As properties go from one party to another, they will want access to the data as they try to re-interpret the data depending on their requirement. Another driver is the building of infrastructure such as pipelines which require this data to incorporate into their engineering specifications.

The Business Model

The simplest is to develop a license agreement with a customer taking a greenfield position in data that is currently under lease. Transactions are a big driver of licensing agreements. In an acquisition/merger, Pulse gives the acquirer 30 days to renegotiate a new deal; in a JV/development a new deal must negotiated and in the case of an asset sale, the licensing agreement does not follow the property to a new owner. These transactions allow Pulse to monetize its data repeatedly and drive revenue from the same assets.

It does not take a lot to manage and administer the business model with administration of contracts and sales being the major drivers, so Pulse supports very healthy margins at roughly 80% of revenue and EbITDA running 76% over the last 5 years.

Financials and Valuation

Pulse posted banner results in 2025. Revenue was $51.1m CAD, EbITDA of $40.8m and shareholder free cash flow (EbITDA less taxes) of $31.6m. These results taken on their own give Pulse some objectively cheap metrics when compared to both the current market cap of $214.3m and enterprise value of $194.6m with an EV to EbITDA under 5x and a price to cash flow of under 7x. This does not really tell the whole story though.

The nature of Pulse’s business is that it has very bulky and volatile revenues, as evidenced below:

The upside is that Pulse has made money every year, even in the trough years and due to its high margins, banner years on the sales front translate directly to the bottom line. For most investors, they like to see steady earnings with regular growth rates. Lumpy earnings like the above make it harder to value.

The company offsets this be returning a substantial amount of capital to its shareholders. This has been primarily done through dividends, but the company also has a buyback in place as well. The regular dividend has been steady since 2021; currently it sits at $0.07 CAD or a 1.7% yield at the current price. However, the company has been doing special dividends most years, with the only exception being 2022 (which was a trough year per the above graph). The 2025 special dividend of $0.20 would bump this yield to over 6% I believe this will continue as the company as 24% insider ownership, making this policy something that clearly aligns with management.

Pulse’s business model is akin to the royalty business models used throughout the natural resource industrial complex, albeit with a more fixed pricing model that is not directly driven by commodity prices. For example, Freehold Royalties (FRU:C) trades at a price to cash flow of 12x and a TTM EV/EbITDA of 12x. This is almost double Pulse’s, but Pulse is performing at near its historical peak financial performance. I do believe that a lot of the reduce multiple and financial performance has been due to the regulatory environment that the natural resource industry has suffered through in Canada over the last decade, but it should be noted that post-COVID, the company’s performance has been trending up.

Looking Forward

It is hard to find a worse regulatory environment for oil and gas than Canada has been over the last decade, despite its natural resource gifts. Development has been impeded at almost every level of government in the country’s pursuit of its environmental goals, despite their small percentage impact compared to many other countries around the world. This has begun to fall with Danielle Smith becoming the new Premier of Alberta which has helped to encourage more exploration and production in Alberta, which has been evident in the up turn in results over the past 5 years. If the Federal government can also start to live up to some of its promises of a more business friendly environment, reduced trade barriers and additional pipelines, Canada could become a much friendlier environment to Pulse’s major customers.

The geopolitical environment has also seen an upturn in oil prices with the uncertainty around the world; energy security is in much greater focus, and higher prices make more properties economic, which would lead to more users of Pulse’s data.

Both these broad macro tailwinds should lead to even better results for Pulse going forward.

Risks

You do not need to look far to see what can happen on the other side of political environment. The disastrous provincial government in British Columbia has brought even basic property rights into question for citizens and business alike, while continuing to throw up roadblocks to pipelines that would benefit Canada as a whole. This is not a large part of Pulse’s business but is certainly not a tailwind.

Artificial intelligence could also be an existential threat if it is able to recreate the data that Pulse has acquired. I suspect that they have very strong data security that keeps this very proprietary information away from LLMs as more companies start to wall off their data in general.

The nature of commodities also means that the other side of the above geopolitical environment means that a soft O&G pricing environment could also impact the amount of activity in the oil patch, which will understandably have a negative impact on Pulse’s financial performance. Fortunately, its cost base gives it a lot of leeway to manage downturns.

The Takeaway

There is a chance for a multiple re-rate if Pulse’s results continue in the trend we saw in 2025, which is certainly possible with a thawing political environment at home in Canada, along with a higher priced oil market driven by current political tensions and potential longer-term effects of the same. Even if a re-rating does not fully occur, the company’s capital return policy will buffer shareholders, who are very aligned with insiders.
